mission.title: Install and Configure Container2wasm on Kubernetes
mission.description: container2wasm is a tool that converts container images into WebAssembly (WASM) format, enabling them to run on WASI runtimes or in the browser. Installing this tool allows you to leverage existing containerized applications in a lightweight, portable format suitable for various environments.

steps.title: Add the container2wasm Helm repository
steps.description:
First, add the container2wasm Helm repository to your local Helm setup. This will allow you to install the container2wasm chart easily.
```bash
helm repo add container2wasm https://ktock.github.io/container2wasm
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install container2wasm using Helm
steps.description:
Now, install the container2wasm chart into your Kubernetes cluster. This command will create a new namespace called 'container2wasm' and deploy the application there.
```bash
helm install container2wasm container2wasm/container2wasm --namespace container2wasm --create-namespace --version 0.8.3
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the container2wasm pods are running successfully in the 'container2wasm' namespace. This command will list all the pods in the specified namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace container2wasm
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
To ensure optimal performance, configure resource limits for the container2wasm deployment. Edit the deployment to set CPU and memory limits.
```bash
kubectl edit deployment container2wasm --namespace container2wasm
# In the editor, add the following under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     memory: "512Mi"
#     cpu: "500m"
```

steps.title: Test the container2wasm functionality
steps.description:
After installation, you can test the functionality of container2wasm by converting a sample container image to WASM. Use the following command to convert an Ubuntu image:
```bash
kubectl exec -it <pod-name> --namespace container2wasm -- c2w ubuntu:22.04 out.wasm
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation will show the container2wasm pods running in the 'container2wasm' namespace, and you will be able to convert container images to WASM format. You can verify the functionality by executing the conversion command and checking for the output file.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add container2wasm https://ktock.github.io/container2wasm
helm repo update

kubectl edit deployment container2wasm --namespace container2wasm
# In the editor, add the following under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     memory: "512Mi"
#     cpu: "500m"
kubectl exec -it <pod-name> --namespace container2wasm -- c2w ubuntu:22.04 out.wasm

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
To cleanly remove the container2wasm deployment, use the following command to uninstall the Helm release from the 'container2wasm' namespace:
```bash
helm uninstall container2wasm --namespace container2wasm
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
If there are any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) or persistent volume claims (PVCs) created by the container2wasm installation, you should clean them up. Use the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd <crd-name>
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace container2wasm
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Finally, remove the 'container2wasm' namespace to clean up all resources associated with it. Verify that the namespace has been deleted:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace container2wasm
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's important to back up the current state of your deployment. You can do this by exporting the current configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all --namespace container2wasm -o yaml > backup-container2wasm.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and then upgrade the container2wasm release to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade container2wasm container2wasm/container2wasm --namespace container2wasm --version 0.8.4
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After the upgrade, verify that the pods are running the new version and are healthy:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace container2wasm
kubectl describe deployment container2wasm --namespace container2wasm
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If your pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs to diagnose the issue:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace container2wasm
```
Common fixes include checking for configuration errors or missing dependencies.

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter a version conflict during an upgrade, check the currently installed version and the available versions:
```bash
helm list --namespace container2wasm
helm search repo container2wasm --versions
```
Ensure you are specifying a valid version in the upgrade command.

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing pod eviction
troubleshooting.description:
If your pods are being evicted due to resource limits, check the events for the namespace:
```bash
kubectl get events --namespace container2wasm
```
You may need to adjust the resource limits in the deployment.

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If the service is not reachable, verify the service configuration and endpoints:
```bash
kubectl get svc --namespace container2wasm
kubectl describe svc <service-name> --namespace container2wasm
kubectl get endpoints --namespace container2wasm
```
Check for any misconfigurations or issues with the pods backing the service.

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher, along with Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
